revision.comment: /* Trees */ adding Gallery heading to match other Shinmei-shrines articles
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = Nagasaki One Legged Torii C1946.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|03|N|129|52|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[hypocentre]] in [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.
==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}
==Notes== {{reflist|2}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sanno Shrine}} [[Category:Monuments and memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Monuments and memorials concerning the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1638]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]]
{{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Hiyoshi Faith}}

==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = Nagasaki One Legged Torii C1946.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|03|N|129|52|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[hypocentre]] in [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.
==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = Nagasaki One Legged Torii C1946.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|03|N|129|52|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[hypocentre]] in [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.
==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = Nagasaki One Legged Torii C1946.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|03|N|129|52|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[hypocentre]] in [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.
==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = Nagasaki One Legged Torii C1946.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|03|N|129|52|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[hypocentre]] in [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.
==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = Nagasaki One Legged Torii C1946.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|03|N|129|52|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[hypocentre]] in [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.
==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = Nagasaki One Legged Torii C1946.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|03|N|129|52|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[hypocentre]] in [[Nagasaki, Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.
==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q211522]] Wikidata name: Sannō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = File:Nagasaki Sanno Shrine 4.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.7675|N|129.86861111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[:en:Hypocentre|Hypocentre]] in [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.
==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q211522]] Wikidata name: Sannō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = File:Nagasaki Sanno Shrine 4.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.7675|N|129.86861111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[:en:Hypocentre|Hypocentre]] in [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.
==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>

The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>

[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q211522]] Wikidata name: Sannō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = File:Nagasaki Sanno Shrine 4.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.7675|N|129.86861111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[:en:Hypocentre|Hypocentre]] in [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.
==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q211522]] Wikidata name: Sannō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = File:Nagasaki Sanno Shrine 4.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.7675|N|129.86861111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[:en:Hypocentre|Hypocentre]] in [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki]], is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.
==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q211522]] Wikidata name: Sannō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = File:Nagasaki Sanno Shrine 4.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.7675|N|129.86861111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[:en:Hypocentre|Hypocentre]] in {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki}}, is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.
==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}
==Notes== {{reflist|2}} <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including--> {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Hiyoshi Faith}}
[[en:Sannō Shrine]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Sannō Shrines (Nagasaki)}} [[fr:Sannō Shrines]] [[it:Santuario di Sannō]] [[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]] [[pl:Sannō Shrines]] [[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]] [[zh:山王神社]] {{wikidata link|Q211522}} [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Monuments and memorials concerning the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki]] [[Category:Monuments and memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1638]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sannō-jinja (Nagasaki)]][[en:Sannō Shrine]][[fa:معبد ساننو]][[fr:Sannō-jinja]][[he:מקדש סאנו]][[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]][[pl:Sannō-jinja]][[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]][[zh:山王神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q211522]] Wikidata name: Sannō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = File:Nagasaki Sanno Shrine 4.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.7675|N|129.86861111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[:en:Hypocentre|Hypocentre]] in {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki}}, is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|}} | image = File:Nagasaki Sanno Shrine 4.jpg | caption = The one-legged ''torii'' at the Sannō Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.7675|N|129.86861111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Sannō Shrine'''|山王神社|Sannō Jinja|literally ''Mountain king shrine''}}, located about 800 metres south-east of the atomic bomb [[:en:Hypocentre|Hypocentre]] in {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki}}, is noted for its one-legged stone ''[[torii]]'' at the shrine entrance.
==Torii== The well-known {{nihongo|one-legged ''torii'' or one-legged arch|一本柱鳥居|}} was one of the unanticipated results of the atomic bomb blast on August 9, 1945.
The epicenter of the bomb's destructive force was located approximately 800 meters from the shrine (in the right background of the image on the left).
One support column was knocked down; but the other somehow remained standing, keeping the gate upright but effectively breaking it in half. The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}
==Notes== {{reflist|2}} <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including--> {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Hiyoshi Faith}}
[[en:Sannō Shrine]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Sannō Shrines (Nagasaki)}} [[fr:Sannō Shrines]] [[it:Santuario di Sannō]] [[ja:山王神社 (長崎市)]] [[pl:Sannō Shrines]] [[ru:Санно-дзиндзя]] [[zh:山王神社]] {{wikidata link|Q211522}} [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Monuments and memorials concerning the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki]] [[Category:Monuments and memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1638]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1869]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

The force of the shockwave rotated the ''torii'' about 30 degrees on its pedestal base. The central part of the shrine is located just behind the photographer of the image on the right.<ref name="museum">[http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html City of Nagasaki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090815060634/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/index_e.html |date=2009-08-15 }}: [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122052354/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/abm/ |date=2012-01-22 }}; [http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html Sannō Shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110210023334/http://www1.city.nagasaki.nagasaki.jp/peace/english/kids/kaisetsu/detail/sanno.html |date=2011-02-10 }}.</ref> ==Trees== The surviving trees of Sannō Shrine have become another living demonstration of destruction and re-growth. Two large [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor tree]]s were scorched, burned and stripped of all leaves by the bomb's shock wave; and yet, despite everything, the trees survived. One tree in Nagasaki was designated a natural monument on February 15, 1969.<ref>Brazil, Mark. [http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fe20020801mb.html "A camphor by any other name,"] ''The Japan Times.'' August 1, 2002.</ref>
The dead parts of the living trees have been enveloped by new growth. <ref name="museum"/> ==Gallery== {{Gallery |width=160 | height=170 |align=center |File:Sanno torii boxed in red.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' of Sannō Shrine is circled in red. |File:Camphor Tree Sanno Shinto Shrine Nagasaki.jpg |Camphor Tree Sannō Shinto Shrine Nagasaki, 2014 |File:Sanno-jinja-afterbomb.jpg |The one-legged ''torii'' and rubble after the atomic blast, 1945 |File:Sanno and trees.jpg |The devastated Sannō camphor trees, 1945 |File:Sanno shrine 1.jpg |View of entrance to the Shrine in 2014 |File:Sanno shrine 2.jpg |View of the Shrine interior in 2014 }}
